annotations.label: Triangle

anchor.snippet: A triangle is a polygon with three corners and three sides

annotations.note: Affine.Triangle abbreviates `Affine.Simplex k P 2`, a bundled triple of affinely independent points.

annotations.label: Sum of angles equals straight angle

anchor.snippet: the sum of angles of a triangle always equals a straight angle

annotations.note: Proves ∠p₁p₂p₃ + ∠p₂p₃p₁ + ∠p₃p₁p₂ = π for three points in a Euclidean affine space.

annotations.label: Area equals half base times height

anchor.snippet: The area of a triangle equals one-half the product of height and base length

annotations.note: No `Affine.Triangle.area` (or simplex analogue with the ½·base·height formula) appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three non-collinear points determine a triangle

anchor.snippet: any three points that do not all lie on the same straight line determine a unique triangle

annotations.note: The structure `Affine.Triangle` bundles exactly three affinely independent (i.e. non-collinear) points.

annotations.label: Tetrahedron

anchor.snippet: four points in three-dimensional Euclidean space determine a solid figure called tetrahedron

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general `Affine.Simplex k P n`; the n = 3 case is the tetrahedron but no dedicated `Tetrahedron` abbreviation exists.

annotations.label: Spherical and hyperbolic triangles (non-Euclidean)

anchor.snippet: three "straight" segments also determine a "triangle", for instance, a spherical triangle or hyperbolic triangle

annotations.note: No definition of spherical or hyperbolic triangle is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Geodesic triangle

anchor.snippet: A geodesic triangle is a region of a general two-dimensional surface enclosed by three sides

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of geodesic triangle on a surface or in a metric space.

annotations.label: Curvilinear triangle

anchor.snippet: A curvilinear triangle is a shape with three curved sides

annotations.note: No definition of curvilinear triangle exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangle (sides and vertices)

anchor.snippet: A triangle is a figure consisting of three line segments, each of whose endpoints are connected

annotations.note: Triangles in Mathlib are bundled as three affinely independent vertices, with `Polygon.toTriangle` linking to the three-segment view.

annotations.label: Equilateral, isosceles, scalene triangles

anchor.snippet: A triangle whose sides are all the same length is an equilateral triangle

annotations.note: `Affine.Simplex.Equilateral` and `Affine.Simplex.Scalene` exist for simplices, but Mathlib has no `Isosceles` predicate.

annotations.label: Right, acute, obtuse triangles

anchor.snippet: A triangle in which one of the angles is a right angle is a right triangle

annotations.note: Only `AcuteAngled` (with `Affine.Triangle.acuteAngled_iff_angle_lt`) is in Mathlib; no `Right` or `Obtuse` triangle predicate.

annotations.label: Deltahedron

anchor.snippet: when polyhedra have all equilateral triangles as their faces, they are known as deltahedra

annotations.note: No deltahedron (or polyhedron-with-equilateral-faces) concept exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simplex (generalized triangle)

anchor.snippet: triangles can be found in higher dimensions, as in the generalized notion of triangles known as the simplex

annotations.note: `Affine.Simplex k P n` is the n-dimensional affine simplex of (n+1) affinely independent points.

annotations.label: Ceva's theorem

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: Ceva's theorem , which gives a criterion for determining when three such lines are concurrent

annotations.note: Mathlib has Ceva's theorem for triangles in several equivalent forms (weights, divisions, distances).

annotations.label: Menelaus' theorem

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: here Menelaus' theorem gives a useful general criterion

annotations.note: Grep over Mathlib finds no occurrence of Menelaus.

annotations.label: Perpendicular bisector

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: A perpendicular bisector of a side of a triangle is a straight line passing through the midpoint of the side

annotations.note: `perpBisector p₁ p₂` is the perpendicular bisector of the segment [p₁, p₂] as a bundled affine subspace.

annotations.label: Circumcenter from perpendicular bisectors

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The three perpendicular bisectors meet in a single point, the triangle's circumcenter

annotations.note: Circumcenter is defined via uniqueness of an equidistant point; the explicit concurrence-of-perpBisectors theorem isn't stated as such.

annotations.label: Circumcircle

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: this point is the center of the circumcircle , the circle passing through all three vertices

annotations.note: `circumsphere` of a simplex specialises to the circumcircle of a triangle.

annotations.label: Thales' theorem (circumcenter on side)

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: Thales' theorem implies that if the circumcenter is located on the side of the triangle, then the angle opposite that side is a right angle

annotations.note: An inscribed angle subtending a diameter is a right angle; aliased as `thales_theorem`.

annotations.label: Circumcenter location and triangle type

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: If the circumcenter is located inside the triangle, then the triangle is acute

annotations.note: No lemma classifying triangle type by interior/exterior position of the circumcenter is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Altitude of a triangle

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: An altitude of a triangle is a straight line through a vertex and perpendicular to the opposite side

annotations.note: Altitude of a simplex is defined as the line through a vertex orthogonal to the opposite face.

annotations.label: Orthocenter from altitudes

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The three altitudes intersect in a single point, called the orthocenter of the triangle

annotations.note: `orthocenter_mem_altitude` plus `eq_orthocenter_of_forall_mem_altitude` show the three altitudes concur at the orthocenter.

annotations.label: Orthocenter inside iff acute

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The orthocenter lies inside the triangle if and only if the triangle is acute

annotations.note: Mathlib has the orthocenter and AcuteAngled but no lemma linking the orthocenter's interior position to acuteness.

annotations.label: Angle bisector

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: An angle bisector of a triangle is a straight line through a vertex that cuts the corresponding angle in half

annotations.note: No dedicated `angleBisector` definition appears in Mathlib's geometry library.

annotations.label: Incenter from angle bisectors

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The three angle bisectors intersect in a single point, the incenter

annotations.note: Incenter is defined as the center of the insphere (tangent to the faces), not via concurrence of angle bisectors.

annotations.label: Incircle

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The incircle is the largest circle that lies inside the triangle; it touches all three sides

annotations.note: `Affine.Simplex.insphere` specialises to the incircle for a triangle.

annotations.label: Excircles and orthocentric system

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: There are three other important circles, the excircles ; they lie outside the triangle and touch one side

annotations.note: `exsphere` covers excircles, and `EuclideanGeometry.OrthocentricSystem` formalises the orthocentric system.

annotations.label: Nine-point circle

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The midpoints of the three sides and the feet of the three altitudes all lie on a single circle, the triangle's nine-point circle

annotations.note: Nine-point circle defined; lemmas like `faceOppositeCentroid_mem_ninePointCircle` and `altitudeFoot_mem_ninePointCircle` verify the nine points.

annotations.label: Nine-point circle radius is half circumradius

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The radius of the nine-point circle is half that of the circumcircle

annotations.note: `ninePointCircle_radius` gives `circumradius / n`, which is circumradius/2 for a triangle (n = 2).

annotations.label: Feuerbach's theorem (nine-point circle touches incircle and excircles)

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: It touches the incircle (at the Feuerbach point ) and the three excircles

annotations.note: Grep for `Feuerbach` returns nothing in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler's line

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: all lie on a single line, known as Euler's line

annotations.note: Circumcenter, centroid and orthocenter all exist but the collinearity result `EulerLine` is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distances along Euler's line

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: the distance between the centroid and the circumcenter is half that between the centroid and the orthocenter

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma stating the 1:2 ratio between centroid–circumcenter and centroid–orthocenter distances.

annotations.label: Median of a triangle

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: A median of a triangle is a straight line through a vertex and the midpoint of the opposite side

annotations.note: `median` is the line from a vertex of a simplex through the centroid of the opposite face; for a triangle this is the median through the midpoint.

annotations.label: Centroid from medians

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The three medians intersect in a single point, the triangle's centroid

annotations.note: States that any point lying on all medians of a simplex equals the centroid.

annotations.label: Centroid is center of mass

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The centroid of a rigid triangular object (cut out of a thin sheet of uniform density) is also its center of mass

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the affine centroid but has no link to a physical centre-of-mass integral over the triangle.

annotations.label: Centroid cuts median 2:1

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: The centroid cuts every median in the ratio 2:1

annotations.note: Commandino's theorem: vertex-to-centroid equals `n` times centroid-to-faceOppositeCentroid, giving the 2:1 ratio when n = 2.

annotations.label: Symmedian and symmedian point

anchor.section: Points, lines, and circles associated with a triangle
anchor.snippet: If one reflects a median in the angle bisector that passes through the same vertex, one obtains a symmedian

annotations.note: No symmedian or symmedian point definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum of interior angles is 180°

anchor.snippet: The sum of the measures of the interior angles of a triangle in Euclidean space is always 180 degrees

annotations.note: Sum of the three angle-at-point measures equals π.

annotations.label: Angle-sum equivalent to parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: This fact is equivalent to Euclid's parallel postulate

annotations.note: Mathlib doesn't axiomatise Euclid's parallel postulate or prove its equivalence to the angle-sum.

annotations.label: Exterior angle theorem

anchor.snippet: The measure of an exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of the measures of the two interior angles that are not adjacent to it

annotations.note: Stated directly using `Sbtw` to place a point on the extension of a side.

annotations.label: Sum of exterior angles is 360°

anchor.snippet: The sum of the measures of the three exterior angles (one for each vertex) of any triangle is 360 degrees

annotations.note: No statement summing the three exterior angles to 2π is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trigonometric functions in right triangles

anchor.snippet: They can be defined as the ratio between any two sides of a right triangle

annotations.note: Mathlib defines sin/cos analytically and has `cos_angle` in terms of inner products, but does not give the right-triangle ratio definition.

annotations.label: Law of sines and cosines

anchor.snippet: methods for doing so use the law of sines and the law of cosines

annotations.note: Both `law_cos` and `law_sin` are proved (with vector-angle variants in InnerProductGeometry).

annotations.label: Three angles summing to 180° form a triangle

anchor.snippet: Any three angles that add to 180° can be the internal angles of a triangle

annotations.note: No converse-existence lemma constructing a triangle from three angles summing to π is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Degenerate triangle

anchor.snippet: A degenerate triangle , whose vertices are collinear , has internal angles of 0° and 180°

annotations.note: `Affine.Triangle` requires affine independence, so a degenerate-triangle predicate is not defined separately.

annotations.label: Similar triangles

anchor.snippet: Two triangles are said to be similar , if every angle of one triangle has the same measure as the corresponding angle in the other triangle

annotations.note: `Similar` is the general indexed-family similarity used by the triangle similarity lemmas in `Mathlib.Geometry.Euclidean.Similarity`.

annotations.label: AA similarity

anchor.snippet: If and only if one pair of internal angles of two triangles have the same measure as each other, and another pair also have the same measure as each other, the triangles are similar

annotations.note: Two pairs of equal angles imply similarity of the two triangles.

annotations.label: SAS similarity

anchor.snippet: If and only if one pair of corresponding sides of two triangles are in the same proportion as another pair of corresponding sides, and their included angles have the same measure, then the triangles are similar

annotations.note: Proportional adjacent sides with equal included angle imply similarity.

annotations.label: SSS similarity

anchor.snippet: If and only if three pairs of corresponding sides of two triangles are all in the same proportion, then the triangles are similar

annotations.note: Proportional sides imply similarity for the (a,b,c)/(a',b',c') triples.

annotations.label: Congruent triangles

anchor.snippet: Two triangles that are congruent have exactly the same size and shape

annotations.note: Triangle congruence is the specialisation of the general `Congruent` predicate, characterised by pairwise equal distances.

annotations.label: SAS congruence postulate

anchor.snippet: SAS Postulate: Two sides in a triangle have the same length as two sides in the other triangle, and the included angles have the same measure

annotations.note: Two sides and the included angle equal ⇒ triangles congruent.

annotations.label: ASA congruence

anchor.snippet: ASA: Two interior angles and the side between them in a triangle have the same measure and length

annotations.note: Two angles and the included side equal ⇒ triangles congruent (with a non-collinearity hypothesis).

annotations.label: SSS congruence

anchor.snippet: SSS: Each side of a triangle has the same length as the corresponding side of the other triangle

annotations.note: Three equal pairs of sides ⇒ congruent, even for degenerate triangles.

annotations.label: AAS congruence

anchor.snippet: AAS: Two angles and a corresponding (non-included) side in a triangle have the same measure and length

annotations.note: Two angles and a non-included side equal ⇒ congruent.

annotations.label: Area as half base times altitude

anchor.snippet: One of the oldest and simplest is to take half the product of the length of one side

annotations.note: No `triangle area = ½·base·height` lemma is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Area from two sides and included angle

anchor.snippet: If two sides

annotations.note: No `½ a b sin C` triangle-area formula in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heron's formula

anchor.snippet: Heron's formula , named after Heron of Alexandria , is a formula for finding the area of a triangle from the lengths of its sides

annotations.note: No Heron's-formula declaration appears in Mathlib (the `Heron`-named hits are all unrelated, e.g. `Harmonic`).

annotations.label: Affine relative area

anchor.snippet: the relative areas of triangles in any affine plane can be defined without reference to a notion of distance or squares

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a notion of relative/affine triangle area.

annotations.label: Shoelace formula for triangle area

anchor.snippet: its relative oriented area can be calculated using the shoelace formula

annotations.note: Grep for `shoelace`/`Shoelace` finds no Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Triangle inequality

anchor.snippet: The triangle inequality states that the sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than or equal to the length of the third side

annotations.note: The metric-space `dist_triangle` is the general form; `norm_add_le` is the vector version.

annotations.label: Converse of triangle inequality

anchor.snippet: some triangle with three given positive side lengths exists if and only if those side lengths satisfy the triangle inequality

annotations.note: No constructive lemma producing a triangle from three side lengths satisfying the triangle inequality is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangle rigidity

anchor.snippet: triangles are sturdy because specifying the lengths of all three sides determines the angles

annotations.note: SSS congruence is the precise statement of triangle rigidity; no separate `rigidity` lemma exists.

annotations.label: Polygon triangulation count

anchor.snippet: In the case of a simple polygon with

annotations.note: Polygon triangulation, Catalan-number counts, and simple-polygon decomposition are not formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two ears theorem

anchor.snippet: The two ears theorem states that every simple polygon that is not itself a triangle has at least two ears

annotations.note: Grep finds no `TwoEar`/`ears`-theorem declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trilinear coordinates

anchor.snippet: Trilinear coordinates specify the relative distances of a point from the sides

annotations.note: Mathlib mentions trilinear coordinates only in a docstring of `SignedDist`; no dedicated definition exists.

annotations.label: Barycentric coordinates

anchor.snippet: Barycentric coordinates of the form

annotations.note: `AffineBasis.coord` gives the ith barycentric coordinate map; lemmas like `coord_apply_combination` characterise it.

annotations.label: Unique incircle

anchor.snippet: every triangle has a unique inscribed circle (incircle) that is interior to the triangle and tangent to all three sides

annotations.note: Mathlib's insphere is uniquely defined for every simplex; specialised to a triangle it gives the unique incircle.

annotations.label: Steiner inellipse

anchor.snippet: Every triangle has a unique Steiner inellipse which is interior to the triangle and tangent at the midpoints of the sides

annotations.note: Grep for `Steiner.*inellipse`/`SteinerInellipse` returns no matches.

annotations.label: Marden's theorem

anchor.snippet: Marden's theorem shows how to find the foci of this ellipse

annotations.note: No `Marden` declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Steiner inellipse has maximum area

anchor.snippet: This ellipse has the greatest area of any ellipse tangent to all three sides of the triangle

annotations.note: Steiner inellipse is absent, so its maximum-area property is also absent.

annotations.label: Mandart inellipse

anchor.snippet: The Mandart inellipse of a triangle is the ellipse inscribed within the triangle tangent to its sides at the contact points of its excircles

annotations.note: Grep for `Mandart` returns no matches.

annotations.label: Pedal triangle and medial triangle

anchor.snippet: From an interior point in a reference triangle, the nearest points on the three sides serve as the vertices of the pedal triangle

annotations.note: Neither `pedalTriangle` nor `medialTriangle` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Medial triangle subdivides into four congruent triangles

anchor.snippet: The midpoint triangle subdivides the reference triangle into four congruent triangles which are similar to the reference triangle

annotations.note: Without a medial-triangle definition, the corresponding subdivision lemma is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intouch triangle

anchor.snippet: The intouch triangle of a reference triangle has its vertices at the three points of tangency of the reference triangle's sides with its incircle

annotations.note: No `intouchTriangle` declaration; only the underlying `Simplex.touchpoint` exists.

annotations.label: Extouch triangle

anchor.snippet: The extouch triangle of a reference triangle has its vertices at the points of tangency of the reference triangle's excircles with its sides

annotations.note: No `extouchTriangle` declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Calabi triangle

anchor.snippet: A notable example of this figure relation is the Calabi triangle in which the vertices of every three squares are tangent to all obtuse triangle's sides

annotations.note: No Calabi triangle definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inscribed squares by triangle type

anchor.snippet: Every acute triangle has three inscribed squares

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma counts inscribed squares of a triangle by type.

annotations.label: Lemoine hexagon

anchor.snippet: The Lemoine hexagon is a cyclic hexagon with vertices given by the six intersections of the sides of a triangle with the three lines that are parallel to the sides

annotations.note: No `Lemoine` hexagon definition is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convex polygon inscribed in triangle area bound

anchor.snippet: Every convex polygon with area

annotations.note: No area-bound theorem comparing inscribed convex polygons to the enclosing triangle is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equality case: inscribed parallelogram

anchor.snippet: Equality holds only if the polygon is a parallelogram

annotations.note: Equality case for the previous (unformalised) inscribed-polygon bound is also absent.

annotations.label: Tangential triangle

anchor.snippet: The tangential triangle of a reference triangle (other than a right triangle) is the triangle whose sides are on the tangent lines to the reference triangle's circumcircle at its vertices

annotations.note: No `tangentialTriangle` definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Steiner circumellipse

anchor.snippet: every triangle has a unique Steiner circumellipse , which passes through the triangle's vertices and has its center at the triangle's centroid

annotations.note: Steiner circumellipse is not defined or characterised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Steiner circumellipse has minimum area

anchor.snippet: Of all ellipses going through the triangle's vertices, it has the smallest area

annotations.note: Without a Steiner circumellipse definition, the minimum-area property is also absent.

annotations.label: Kiepert hyperbola

anchor.snippet: The Kiepert hyperbola is unique conic that passes through the triangle's three vertices, its centroid, and its circumcenter

annotations.note: No Kiepert hyperbola is formalised in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximum-area triangle in convex polygon

anchor.snippet: Of all triangles contained in a given convex polygon , one with maximal area can be found in linear time

annotations.note: Algorithmic result on maximum-area inscribed triangles is not part of Mathlib.

annotations.label: Circular triangle

anchor.snippet: A circular triangle is a triangle with circular arc edges

annotations.note: No circular-triangle definition appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intersection of three disks is a convex circular triangle

anchor.snippet: The intersection of three disks forms a circular triangle whose sides are all convex

annotations.note: No lemma describing the intersection of three disks as a circular triangle is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reuleaux triangle

anchor.snippet: An example of a circular triangle with three convex edges is a Reuleaux triangle

annotations.note: Grep for `Reuleaux` returns no matches.

annotations.label: Mohr–Mascheroni theorem

anchor.snippet: The construction may be performed with a compass alone without needing a straightedge, by the Mohr–Mascheroni theorem

annotations.note: No `Mohr` declaration in Mathlib (the `Mascheroni` hits are about the Euler–Mascheroni constant).

annotations.label: Pseudotriangle

anchor.snippet: A pseudotriangle is a simply-connected subset of the plane lying between three mutually tangent convex regions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of pseudotriangle.

annotations.label: Pseudo-triangulation counts

anchor.snippet: disks in a pseudotriangle, the partition gives

annotations.note: Pseudo-triangulation counts are unformalised.

annotations.label: Convex hull of pseudotriangle is a triangle

anchor.snippet: The convex hull of any pseudotriangle is a triangle

annotations.note: Without a pseudotriangle definition, no convex-hull lemma about them is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic and spherical triangles

anchor.snippet: A triangle in hyperbolic space is called a hyperbolic triangle

annotations.note: No hyperbolic or spherical triangle definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Angle sum in hyperbolic and spherical triangles

anchor.snippet: the sum of the internal angles of a hyperbolic triangle is less than 180°, and for any spherical triangle, the sum is more than 180°

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalise angle sums in hyperbolic or spherical triangles.

annotations.label: Girard's theorem

anchor.snippet: By Girard's theorem , the sum of the angles of a triangle on a sphere is

annotations.note: No Girard-theorem (spherical excess) declaration is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangle comparison theorems

anchor.snippet: there are comparison theorems relating the properties of a triangle in the space to properties of a corresponding triangle in a model space

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Toponogov-style triangle comparison theorems.

annotations.label: CAT(k) space

anchor.snippet: a CAT(k) space is characterized by such comparisons

annotations.note: No CAT(k) space definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fractal triangles (Sierpiński, Koch)

anchor.snippet: Fractal shapes based on triangles include the Sierpiński gasket and the Koch snowflake

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Sierpiński gasket or Koch snowflake construction.
